title: Choosing a Route 53 Routing Policy
description:
## Lowest Latency Across Active Regions

A company runs the **same application in three Regions** (`us-east-1`, `eu-west-1`, `ap-southeast-1`), all **active and healthy**. They want each user's DNS resolution to send them to the Region that gives that user the **lowest network latency**, which is not always the geographically closest one. Health checks should pull a Region out of rotation if it becomes unhealthy.

Which **Route 53 routing policy** should they configure?

options.text: **Latency-based routing**, with one record per Region and an associated health check on each.
**Geolocation routing**, mapping each continent to its nearest Region.
**Weighted routing**, sending an equal one-third of traffic to each Region.
**Failover routing**, with one Region primary and the others as secondaries.

explanation: **Latency-based routing** answers each DNS query with the Region that offers the **lowest measured network latency** to the requesting resolver — which is exactly the stated goal, and notably *not* necessarily the geographically nearest one. Combined with **health checks**, an unhealthy Region is automatically removed from candidate answers. Option B, **geolocation routing**, decides purely by the *location* of the user (continent/country), which can route someone to a higher-latency Region if the network path is poor; it answers "where is the user" not "which is fastest." Option C, **weighted routing**, splits traffic by fixed proportions (useful for blue/green or canary), ignoring latency entirely. Option D, **failover routing**, is active-passive (one primary, standby secondary) and would leave two healthy Regions idle, contradicting the all-active requirement.

hints.title: Latency is measured, location is assumed
hints.body: Latency-based routing reacts to actual network performance; geolocation only knows where the user is. They are not the same thing.

hints.title: All Regions active
hints.body: If every Region should serve traffic when healthy, rule out failover (active-passive) immediately.
